title: Ethics in AI: Algorithmic Bias & Diverse Data
subtitle: Building fair, balanced, and inclusive intelligent systems!
overview: In this crucial conceptual and discussion-based session, students explore the ethical boundaries of Machine Learning. They examine how algorithmic bias creeps into AI models, investigate real-world impacts of unrepresentative data, and discover why diverse data pools are essential to engineering fair software.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — student debate panels, sample biased-classification printouts on boards, and whiteboard mind-maps.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Whiteboard diagram showing the loop of biased historical data training a biased model which creates more biased data
gallery.caption: The Algorithmic Feedback Loop — Garbage In, Garbage Out!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Chart showing a dataset missing key demographic slices
gallery.caption: Data Imbalance — When an AI is trained on only one type of voice, face, or perspective.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Photo of students split into groups acting out an 'AI Ethics Board' testing a hiring algorithm
gallery.caption: Students roleplaying as an AI Ethics Review Board reviewing automated decisions.

keyTopics: What is AI Bias? — When a computer model consistently makes unfair or prejudiced decisions against certain groups
The Source of Bias — Machine Learning learns strictly from historical human data; if human history is biased, the AI clones it
Underrepresentation — Why a facial recognition system fails when its training photos lack diverse skin tones and features
Echo Chambers & Feedback Loops — How biased automated choices reinforce real-world stereotypes over time
The Diversity Mandate — Why gathering varied, inclusive datasets creates accurate, internationally capable technology
Algorithmic Auditing — Actively checking and checking again to find hidden mathematical imbalances before deploying code
Inclusive Engineering Teamwork — Why diverse human engineering groups naturally anticipate and fix algorithmic traps early
Ethical Frameworks — Establishing basic ground rules: Accountability, Transparency, Fairness, and Privacy

resources.title: Teachable Machine — Test Your Own Dataset Balance

activity.title: The AI Ethics Board Audit Challenge
parts: Scenario Cards (e.g., 'An automated college admission AI rejecting students who mention specific hobbies')
Data Profile sheets (showing 90% male resumes and 10% female resumes used for a tech hiring bot)
steps: Setup: Divide the class into 'AI Audit Panels' tasked with assessing a newly proposed automation software tool.
The Disclosure: Hand out the data profile sheet showing where the engineering team got their training samples.
Root Cause Discovery: Teams highlight where the training data lacks balance (e.g., 'All face samples were collected under direct studio lighting from only one country').
Impact Mapping: Students deduce who gets hurt or unfairly rejected if this flawed tool runs in production.
The Correction Strategy: Teams rewrite the data collection guidelines, mapping exactly how to source balanced data and fix the algorithm!

quiz.q: Where does a Machine Learning model get its bias from?
options: The computer's hardware wires overheating
Imbalances, gaps, or human prejudices present inside the historical data used to train it
The internet router connection speed
The AI choosing to be mean on purpose
quiz.explanation: Data Mirrors History! AI models don't possess independent human emotions; they act like a mirror to whatever training information humans feed them. Flawed data creates a flawed model.

quiz.q: If an AI sorting tool evaluates software engineer resumes using old data from an era where only men were hired, what will it likely do?
options: Automatically fix historical injustices
Unfairly down-rate or reject applications from women because its training data implies tech workers are exclusively male
Delete all resumes to save space
Stop working and ask for help
quiz.explanation: Cloning the Past! Because the data teaches the computer that successful past hires matched a certain demographic, it copies that exact pattern and punishes clean, highly qualified outside candidates.

quiz.q: What happens when an AI dataset is 'Underrepresentative'?
options: The file sizes are too small to open
It lacks diverse data or leaves out key groups of people, leading to broken performance for those skipped groups
It trains the model much faster than normal
The system becomes perfectly fair
quiz.explanation: Exclusion Errors! If a voice recognition system is trained entirely on adult male accents, it will fail to accurately understand children or diverse regional accents.

quiz.q: Why does diverse training data matter in modern AI systems?
options: It ensures the system behaves accurately, safely, and fairly for people across different backgrounds, accents, and skin tones
It makes the computer use less electricity
It changes the appearance of the code window
It allows the AI to run without any source code
quiz.explanation: Inclusivity Equals Accuracy! A wide, varied dataset trains the mathematical parameters to notice patterns across all groups, preventing blind spots.

quiz.q: What is the goal of an 'Algorithmic Audit'?
options: To buy newer, faster computer processors
To actively check, challenge, and test a model's outputs for unfair patterns and bias before it goes live
To clear out temporary internet cookies
To translate the program into ancient languages
quiz.explanation: Quality Defense! Auditing means proactively stress-testing the neural network against different demographics to spot hidden mathematical skews.

quiz.q: What term describes the system loop where an unfair AI output generates more biased real-world data, which then reinforces the bad AI?
options: Data compression
Algorithmic Feedback Loop
Battery drain cycle
Memory leak
quiz.explanation: The Feedback Loop! When a biased algorithm makes choices, those choices create new skewed historical data points. Sourcing future versions on this data traps the system in a self-fulfilling prophecy.

quiz.q: Which practice helps prevent bias during the creation of an AI application?
options: Hiding the training data from the engineers
Employing a diverse engineering group and collecting wide, thoroughly balanced datasets
Only testing the software on the team lead's computer
Turning off all warning labels in the code
quiz.explanation: Multi-Layered Protection! Combining a diverse developer team (who spot issues from unique perspectives) with an inclusive dataset keeps algorithmic bias from slipping through undetected.
